title: Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow Agents
subtitle: Four review agents, bundled into the harness, that check every application for quality, consistency, and security before it ships.
abstract: If AI writes and repairs the code, something has to check the AI. Four review agents ride inside the harness, each nicknamed for a colour. Green checks code quality and hygiene. Yellow checks how the writing reads. Red attacks the application the way an outsider would. Blue defends it, judging the code against an international security standard and turning the evidence into a verdict a decision-maker can act on. These agents are bundled into the harness as checks and balances: a piece of work cannot be called done until it passes. Security and quality stop being something we chase after the fact and become first-class concerns.

hero_image.alt: One application at the centre, surrounded by four review agents coloured green, yellow, red, and blue, forming a balanced protective seal.
hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial cover illustration of a single application or building at the centre, surrounded by four distinct review agents arranged in balance around it, one tinted green, one yellow, one red, one blue, together forming a protective seal or set of checks and balances. A sense of four disciplines guarding one piece of work in concert. Muted Alberta cream base with clear green, amber-yellow, rust-red, and navy-blue accents, archival print quality, no text, no logos.

slides.subcaption: Four review agents that keep every build honest.
slides.text: Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow Agents. If AI writes and repairs the code, something has to check the AI. That something is more AI, of a particular kind, pointed back at the work. Four review agents ride inside the harness, each named for a colour, and they inspect every application for quality, consistency, and security before it ships.

slides.text: Green is the foundation, and it runs first. It reports the plain facts of the code's health: vulnerable dependencies, secrets left in the source, thin test coverage, gaps in the build pipeline. It is deterministic, so the same code always returns the same findings. It does not guess; it counts. That is what makes it the evidence base every other agent reads.

slides.text: Yellow reads the writing: documentation, interface copy, the messages a citizen sees. When AI writes prose it leaves tells, and Yellow walks twelve rules across all of it, flagging each tell with a plain rewrite. These are the same rules that govern the papers you are reading. It runs in seconds, and it keeps the whole estate sounding like a person wrote it.

slides.text: Red is the attacker. It studies a finished application from the outside, the way an adversary would, and maps its exposed surface. Then Claude reasons over what it found and plans how it would break in, proposing concrete attack paths. The reconnaissance is repeatable tooling; the attack plan is judgment. Red finds the hole before someone else does.

slides.text: Blue is the defender, and it runs last because it reads everything the others found. It builds a threat model, walks every requirement of the international security standard at Level 2 with the evidence behind each call, folds in Red's reconnaissance, and writes the report a decision-maker can act on. Where Green counts and Red probes, Blue judges.

slides.title: Checks and balances
slides.caption: Checks and balances

visual_config.stat_label: Major security controls in the secure template — verified by 400+ individual checks every pass

slides.subcaption: Bundled into the harness as gates. A piece of work passes only when it clears them all.
slides.text: Apart, each agent is useful. Together they are checks and balances, bundled into the harness as gates. The harness will not call a piece of work done if the security scan shows a critical finding, if the scan is stale, or if the review never ran. The gates are mechanical, not aspirational. This is how the cyber problem is answered without patching forever, and how the build methods stay safe at speed. Security and quality become a property of how the code is made.

sections.title: Four agents, one system of checks and balances
What it runs on: Claude, the Agent SDK, and Vertex
Appendix: the complete inventory of checks

blocks.text: The earlier papers set out two halves of the same problem. We can now find what is wrong across the whole estate, from the [rising cyber pressure](/paper/mwo98) to the health of every repository, and we have [four ways to rebuild it](/paper/zgym1). Both halves lean on AI doing the work. That raises the obvious question. If an AI writes or repairs the code, who checks the AI? Four review agents, each named for a colour, ride inside [the harness](/paper/p7p2k) and inspect every application for quality, consistency, and security. They are the checks and balances on everything the builders produce.

blocks.title: Who checks the AI

blocks.text: The colours come from security practice, where red is the attacker and blue is the defender. We added two more. Green covers code quality and hygiene. Yellow covers how the content reads. Each colour is a separate agent and looks at the work from an angle the others do not. A developer runs each agent throughout the development lifecycle process and at every milestone in the project.

The four are split on purpose between mechanical and judgment work. Green and Yellow are fully deterministic: give them the same code and they return the same findings every time. Red's reconnaissance is deterministic too, with one step that uses Claude to plan how an attack would go. Blue is mostly judgment, the kind a senior reviewer brings. Determinism is what lets an agent act as a gate that never drifts. Judgment is what catches the problem a checklist would miss. The harness uses each where it fits.

blocks.title: The four review agents at a glance
rows: Code quality, dependencies, committed secrets, test coverage, the build pipeline.
First. The evidence base the others read.
Every piece of writing in the project, for the tells of machine-made prose.
In parallel, before anything is published.
The finished application from an outside attacker's point of view.
Deterministic recon and DAST scanners; attack-planning by Claude.
The defensive posture, against OWASP ASVS Level 2 (285 requirements) and Alberta's 62 cloud-security rules.
Mostly judgment, built on the others' evidence.
Last. It reads Green and Red and writes the verdict.
blocks.caption: Four disciplines, four agents, one piece of work. Three are deterministic enough to act as gates; Blue brings the expert read.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness, four-team review framework.

blocks.title: Green: quality and hygiene

blocks.text: Green is the foundation, and it runs first. It reads the code and reports the plain facts of its health. Which dependencies carry known vulnerabilities. Whether a password or key has been committed into the source. Whether dangerous patterns are present. How much of the code the tests actually cover. It works in two rounds: the first reads the code without running it, the second runs the existing tests and watches what happens, including which parts of the code the tests never touch.

Because Green is deterministic, the same code always produces the same findings. That is what makes it the evidence base every other agent builds on. It does not guess; it counts. When it flags a file with almost no test coverage, or a secret sitting in the source, that finding is a fact rather than an opinion, and it can be checked the same way twice.

blocks.title: Green: the plain facts of the code's health
blocks.caption: Green inspects the code itself: vulnerable dependencies, committed secrets, dangerous patterns, thin test coverage, and gaps in the build pipeline. Deterministic by design, so the same code returns the same findings every time.

image.alt: A green-tinted inspector methodically checking the parts of a machine against a checklist, finding a loose component.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration, green-accented, of a meticulous inspector going part by part over the inner workings of a machine or engine, ticking items on a clipboard checklist and pulling out one faulty bolt and one exposed key. A sense of patient, exact, repeatable code hygiene. Muted Alberta cream base with a clear green accent, archival print quality, no text. This must be related to software development, the engine an analogy to the software. No legend.

blocks.title: Yellow: how the content reads

blocks.text: Yellow reads the application content. Government software carries a lot of prose: documentation, the copy inside the interface, release notes, the messages a citizen actually sees. When AI writes that prose it leaves tells, the turns of phrase that mark text as machine-made and quietly erode trust. These tells are often called 'AI smell'. Yellow walks twelve rules across every piece of writing in the project and flags each tell with the offending line, a plain rewrite, and a short note on why it reads as a machine wrote it.

These are the same rules that govern the papers you are reading now. An em dash, a stock cliché, a fawning opening, an "ensure" standing in for a real verb: each is caught and named. The point is simple. If the public is going to read it, it should read like a person wrote it. Yellow is the cheapest of the four to run, a matter of seconds, and it keeps the whole estate sounding human.

blocks.title: Yellow: writing that reads like a person wrote it
blocks.caption: Yellow runs twelve rules across all the prose in a project, flagging the tells of machine-made text and proposing a plain rewrite for each. The same rules that govern these papers.

image.alt: A yellow-tinted editor's pass over a page of text, circling tell-tale phrases and writing a cleaner line in the margin.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration, amber-yellow accented, of a careful editor passing over a page of text, circling a few tell-tale phrases and writing a cleaner, plainer line in the margin. A sense of prose being made to sound human again. Muted Alberta cream base with a clear amber-yellow accent, archival print quality, no text. Write the most cliche em dash filled AI cliche and show someone crossing this stuff off with a red pen.

blocks.title: Red: the attacker

blocks.text: Red is the attacker. It looks at a finished application the way an outsider with bad intentions would, from the outside in. It maps the domain and its subdomains, scans for open ports, checks the encryption and the security headers, and works out which technologies are in use. That reconnaissance is ordinary, repeatable tooling, and it runs on any machine without special setup.

Then Red does the part that used to need a specialist. It reasons over everything it found and plans how it would break in, proposing concrete attack paths: slipping past a login, chaining a remote exploit, drawing data out a side door. In that planning step, Claude does the thinking, and it is the one part of Red that varies from run to run. Red only ever runs against systems the government owns and has authorized for testing. Its job is to find the hole before someone else does, and to hand the next agent a map of how an attack would actually unfold. It is the adversary that helps knock down your application long before it makes it out into the world.

blocks.title: Red: thinks like an adversary
blocks.caption: Red studies a finished application from the outside, maps its exposed surface, then plans concrete attack paths from what it finds. Reconnaissance is repeatable tooling; the attack plan is where Claude reasons.

image.alt: A red-accented security analyst studying a stylized application drawn as a fortress and network diagram, tracing test probes against its outer wall and marking exposed entry points.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration, rust-red accented, of a security analyst at a desk in daylight studying a stylized application drawn as a small fortress and network diagram, tracing authorized test-probe lines against its outer wall and marking the exposed entry points on a chart. A professional scene conveying authorized offensive security testing, mapping where a system is weak so it can be strengthened. Muted Alberta cream base with a clear rust-red accent, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.title: Blue: the defender

blocks.text: Blue is the defender, and it runs last because it reads everything the others found. It builds a map of the application, classifies how sensitive each part is, and writes a threat model. Then it walks every requirement in the OWASP Application Security Verification Standard at Level 2, the international baseline for a serious web application, and records each one as pass, fail, or not applicable, with the evidence behind the call. It checks the same code against Alberta's cybersecurity architecture standard, folds Red's reconnaissance into a step-by-step picture of how an attack would unfold, and writes security tests that prove each fix holds.

Blue's last act is to write the report a decision-maker can act on. An executive summary. The findings ranked by severity. The standards met and missed. A remediation plan that points to the exact files. Where Green counts and Red probes, Blue judges, the way a senior security architect would, and turns a pile of evidence into a defensible verdict that an auditor can follow.

blocks.title: Blue: turns evidence into a verdict
blocks.caption: Blue reads the deterministic evidence and the attack map, checks the code against an international standard and Alberta's architecture standard, and writes the ranked, evidence-backed report leadership and auditors rely on.

image.alt: A blue-tinted architect inside, assembling the findings into a defensive map and a clear report, raising a shield over the building.
image.image_prompt: A wise security mentor, consolidating the findings from the yellow, green, red agents, and the full blue agent analysis, assembling them into one clear defensive map. These reports tie together 95 different key insights which form the security analysis report, kill chain analysis, and remediation plan. A sense of evidence being turned into a calm, authoritative verdict. Muted Alberta cream base with a clear navy-blue accent, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.title: Four agents, one system of checks and balances

blocks.text: On their own, each agent is useful. Together they are a system of checks and balances. Green and Yellow run first and fast, side by side. Red attacks from the outside. Blue takes the deterministic evidence from Green and the attack picture from Red and renders the judgment. The deterministic agents give gates that never regress; Blue gives the expert read a checklist alone would miss. No single agent could do all jobs, so the work is divided four ways and recombined at the end.

What turns these reviews from advice into enforcement is that they are bundled into the harness as gates. The harness will not let a piece of work be called done if the security scan shows a critical finding, if the scan is stale, or if the review never ran. Every "must do" is backed by a check that fails loudly when it is skipped. The gates are mechanical, not aspirational.

blocks.title: What the gates actually enforce
columns: The harness blocks the work when…
rows: A critical security finding is still open.
the security gate, checked before a session can end
The security scan is missing, or more than a day old.
A must-have requirement has no actual code behind it.
A database change cannot be safely re-run.
A fixed problem has no new rule to stop it returning.
blocks.caption: Each gate is a check that fails loudly when its condition is not met, so a review cannot be skipped by hand-waving. A blocked gate is fixed or formally re-planned, never bypassed.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness, mechanical gate scripts.

blocks.text: Across fourteen ASVS chapters and ten Alberta cloud-security domains, from authentication and session handling to input validation and supply-chain hygiene. A full pass runs more than four hundred individual checks, the estate gets a little safer each time, and the lesson is kept rather than relearned.

blocks.label: Major security controls built into the secure template
blocks.body: The four review agents verify these and far more — over four hundred individual checks on every pass: 285 ASVS Level 2 requirements, 62 Alberta cloud-security rules, plus the Green, Yellow, and Red checks.

blocks.title: The four working in concert
blocks.caption: Green and Yellow check the work, Red attacks it, Blue defends and judges. Bundled into the harness as gates, the four reach one verdict, and the application passes only when it clears them all.

image.alt: Four review agents coloured green, yellow, red, and blue working in concert around one application as it passes through a checkpoint gate.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of four review agents, one green, one amber-yellow, one rust-red, one navy-blue, working in concert and in balance around a single application as it passes through a checkpoint or gate. Each agent contributes its own check and the four combine into one verdict. A sense of harmonious checks and balances guarding one piece of work. Muted Alberta cream base with clear green, amber-yellow, rust-red, and navy-blue accents, archival print quality, no text, no logos.

blocks.text: Security and quality stop being something we chase after the fact, and become a property of how the code is made.
blocks.cite: Paper 7 · Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow Agents

blocks.text: The apps produced in concern with these four agents address the critical challenges raised within [The Cyber Imperative](/paper/mwo98). The build methods from [The Four Approaches](/paper/zgym1) are safe to run at speed. The builders get their speed, and the public gets code that has been attacked, defended, and proof-read before it ever reaches them.

blocks.title: What it runs on: Claude, the Agent SDK, and Vertex

blocks.text: All of this runs on the Anthropic Claude platform. The deterministic agents, Green, Yellow, and Red's reconnaissance, are ordinary code that needs no model at all. The parts that call for judgment, Red's attack planner and Blue's whole assessment, are built with the Claude Agent SDK, the toolkit for running a model through many steps with a fixed set of tools and a budget of turns. The harness pins which model does which job and how hard it is allowed to work, so a review is repeatable and its cost is known before it starts.

Alberta runs these models through Claude on Google Vertex AI, now the Google Agent Platform. That keeps the work inside a governed Canadian cloud, with the model provider held behind a clean boundary so it can be changed as the field moves. The same boundary lets a local or open model be tested against the commercial one on identical work, which matters as the economics shift. The agents do not care which model answers, only that the answer meets the standard.

blocks.title: What this gives the builders

blocks.text: The four agents are how Alberta makes "the AI built it" a sentence a security reviewer can trust. They catch what a tired person would miss, they never skip a step, and they turn quality and security from a hope into a gate the code has to pass. The builders move faster because the safety net is automatic and always on, and because a found problem becomes a permanent rule rather than a recurring surprise.

With the harness and its review agents in place, the question turns to the factory that puts them to work on every build. The next papers open up that factory: where applications are [designed and specified](/paper/qthji), where agents build and test them under these same controls, and where the work is tracked and measured.

blocks.title: Appendix: the complete inventory of checks

blocks.text: The body of this paper describes four agents. The full surface is larger, and worth setting out in plain sight. Every application is built on a secure template of roughly **ninety-five major security controls** (see [Anatomy of a Template](/paper/l5wsi)). The four review agents verify that template and far more: audited against the harness source, a complete pass runs **more than four hundred individual checks** — 285 OWASP ASVS Level 2 requirements, 62 Alberta cloud-security rules, thirty Green scanners, twelve Yellow rules, and twenty-three Red tools and stages. Green, Yellow, and Red's reconnaissance and scanners are deterministic and act as gates that never drift; Red's exploitation planner and most of Blue are probabilistic, the expert read a checklist would miss. The full inventory follows.

blocks.title: Green — code quality and supply-chain hygiene (30 checks, all deterministic)
rows: API base URL drift across frontend, tests, CI, env templates, and Go defaults.
Local file: path dependencies that fail outside the monorepo.
Missing security gates (SAST, dep audit, secret scan, build, test) in CI workflows.
console.* debug calls left in production source.
SAST for innerHTML/eval/document.write/dangerouslySetInnerHTML and Go SQL string-building.
Risky defaults in committed .env files (devtools on, debug, placeholders).
ESLint config anti-patterns (blanket disable, missing rules).
.gitignore covers env, build artifacts, node_modules, coverage.
Build-tag test files that disable auth; verifies prod cannot carry the bypass.
Low Go test coverage, stricter for auth/security packages.
go.mod go/toolchain directives; capitalized error strings.
.golangci.yml present and enabling the expected linters.
Reachable Go vulnerabilities with the import trace.
Counts //go:build integration suites so orphaned tests are visible.
SAST for Java/JSP: SQLi, XXE, weak crypto, TLS/host-verifier bypass, XSS.
GPL/AGPL/LGPL copyleft packages in npm trees.
Gaps in numbered SQL migration sequences.
npm audit advisories per package tree.
Packages with no enforced formatting standard.
SAST for Python: eval/exec, pickle, shell=True, SQLi, weak hash, debug modes.
Credentials and API keys across the tree (~10 provider patterns).
Source files over 100 lines with under 20% coverage.
Vue/TS type errors the build would not fail on.
WebSocket handlers: rate limiting, error leakage, unauthenticated messages.
blocks.caption: Each scanner runs in its own subprocess and stays quiet on stacks it does not apply to. Green is the deterministic evidence base every other agent reads. Several checks bundle sub-items — secret_scan alone covers about ten credential types.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.title: Yellow — writing and AI-smell (12 rules, all deterministic)
rows: "not X, but Y" constructions — the strongest single AI-prose tell (6 patterns).
Em dashes and en dashes in prose.
Four-part parallel structures used as flourish.
Runs of short declarative sentences for dramatic effect.
"this is the moment / this is where / this is how" anchors (3 patterns).
Banned words, openers, clichés, metaphor words (~45 patterns).
"X, Y, and Z" lists used as flourish.
Sentences closing with a comma + -ing phrase that restate the sentence.
Vague intensifiers (very, really, truly, extremely, …) — 10 words.
Five composite tics: sycophancy, recap, over-explanation, hedge-stacks, "as an AI".
blocks.caption: Twelve regex and heuristic rules over all prose in the project, expanding to more than sixty distinct sub-patterns. The same rules govern these papers.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.title: Red — adversarial recon, scanning, and exploitation (23 tools and stages)
rows: DNS record enumeration, optional AXFR / DNSSEC.
Audits 6 response headers (HSTS, CSP, X-CTO, X-Frame, Referrer-Policy…).
TCP port scan (nmap or Node fallback).
Subdomains via subfinder, crt.sh, and a bruteforce wordlist.
Service/version port scan with banner grab.
Parses the ZAP report to JSON (alerts, severities, CWEs).
LLM planner; four stages (recon synthesis → code analysis → PoC → recommendation); plans auth-bypass / RCE / exfil paths.
Validates the code-analysis deliverable (schema, attack-surface coverage).
Validates the PoC deliverable (structure, severity taxonomy, chains).
Validates the remediation deliverable (ranking, affected files, coverage).
LLM-as-judge scoring of deliverable quality against criteria.
blocks.caption: Recon and the active scanners are deterministic tooling; the exploitation planner reasons with Claude across four internal stages; the output-eval stages grade Red's own deliverables. Red runs only against systems the government owns and has authorized for testing.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.title: Blue — defensive review and compliance (15 phases)
rows: Maps stack, endpoints, auth, secrets, trust boundaries.
Classifies each component by data sensitivity; assigns controls.
Architecture review: auth model, sessions, secrets, boundaries.
STRIDE threat model per asset, with mitigations.
Walks 285 ASVS L2 requirement checks; pass/fail/N-A with evidence.
Walks 62 Alberta cloud-security (CAS) rules; same shape as ASVS.
Folds Blue + Red findings into a Lockheed kill chain.
Orchestrates the deterministic scanners (npm audit, secretlint, ESLint-security, OSV).
Generates security unit tests per ASVS requirement.
Disaster-recovery and resilience: failure modes, recovery objectives, backups.
Proposes a unified-diff patch per finding.
Composes the ranked report: summary, kill chain, ASVS/CAS, roadmap.
Maps business/functional/non-functional requirements onto the app map.
Generates ASVS-L2-compliant code with inline requirement refs.
Generates CAS-compliant code with inline rule refs.
blocks.caption: Blue runs last and reads everything the others produced. Most phases are LLM judgement over deterministic evidence; phases 05 and 06 are the two standards walks enumerated below.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.title: Blue phase 05 — OWASP ASVS Level 2, by chapter (285 requirement checks)
blocks.caption: Phase 05 walks every requirement of the OWASP Application Security Verification Standard at Level 2. Applicability varies with the application; file-upload, GraphQL, SOAP, and unmanaged-language requirements are recorded as not-applicable when the feature is absent. Every requirement is listed in full below.
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.title: Blue phase 06 — Alberta cloud-security (CAS) rules (62, by domain)
rows: No Secrets or PII in Logs
blocks.caption: Phase 06 walks Alberta's cloud application security baseline — 62 rules across 10 domains, several mapping to ITSG-33 control families. Rule LOG-001 alone carries eleven sub-requirements (a–k).
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.title: Blue phase 05 — the full OWASP ASVS Level 2 checklist (285 requirements)
rows: The use of a secure software development lifecycle that addresses security in all stages
The use of threat modeling for every design change or sprint planning to identify threats, plan for countermeasures, facilitate appropriate risk responses, and guide security testing
All user stories and features contain functional security constraints
Documentation and justification of all the application's trust boundaries, components, and significant data flows
Definition and security analysis of the application's high-level architecture and all remote services
Implementation of centralized, simple (economy of design), vetted, secure, and reusable security controls to avoid duplicate, missing, inconsistent, or fragile controls
Availability of a secure coding checklist, security requirements, guideline, or policy to all developers
The use of unique or special low-privilege operating system accounts for all application components, services, and servers
Communications between application components, including APIs, middleware and data layers, are authenticated
Uses a single vetted authentication mechanism that is known to be strong, can be extended to include strong authentication, and has sufficient logging and monitoring to detect account abuse or…
All authentication pathways and identity management APIs implement consistent authentication security control strength, such that there are no weaker alternatives that can be used to break the…
Uses a single vetted session management mechanism that is known to be strong, can be extended to include strong authentication
Trusted enforcement points such as access control gateways, servers, and serverless functions enforce access controls
A single and auditable access control mechanism is used by the application
Data and functionality are protected based on security classification of the data being handled
Input and output requirements clearly define how to handle and process data based on type, content, and applicable laws, regulations, and other policy compliance
Serialization is not used when communicating with untrusted clients
Input validation is enforced on a trusted service layer, not on the client alone
Output encoding occurs close to or by the interpreter for which it is intended
There is an explicit policy for management of cryptographic keys and that a cryptographic key lifecycle follows a key management standard such as NIST SP 800-57
Consumers of cryptographic services protect key material and other secrets by using key vaults or API-based alternatives
All keys and passwords are replaceable and are part of a well-defined process to re-encrypt sensitive data
The architecture treats client-side secrets as visible to attackers
A common logging format and approach is used across the system
Logs are securely transmitted to a preferably remote system for analysis, detection, alerting, and escalation
All sensitive data is identified and classified into protection levels
All protection levels have an associated set of protection requirements
The application encrypts communications between components, particularly when these components are in different containers, systems, sites, or cloud providers
Application components verify the authenticity of each side in a communication link to prevent person-in-the-middle attacks
A source code control system is in use, with procedures to ensure that check-ins are accompanied by issues or change tickets
The definition and documentation of all application components in terms of the business or security functions they provide
All high-value business logic flows, including authentication, session management and access control, do not share unsynchronized state
All high-value business logic flows, including authentication, session management and access control, are thread-safe and resistant to time-of-check and time-of-use race conditions
User-uploaded files are stored outside the web root or in a cloud storage bucket with separate access policies
User-uploaded files — if required to be displayed or downloaded from the application — are served by either octet stream downloads, or from an unrelated domain, such as a cloud file storage bucket
All application components use the same encoding and escaping strategies to avoid attacks
The segregation of components of differing trust levels through well-defined security controls, firewall rules, API gateways, reverse proxies, cloud-based security groups, or similar mechanisms
Binary signatures, trusted connections, and verified endpoints are used to deploy binaries to remote devices
The build pipeline warns of out-of-date or insecure components and takes appropriate actions
The build pipeline contains a build step to automatically build and verify the secure deployment of the application, particularly if the application infrastructure is software defined, such as cloud…
Application deployments adequately sandbox, containerize and/or isolate at the network level to delay and deter attackers from attacking other applications
Does not use unsupported, insecure, or deprecated client-side technologies such as NSAPI plugins, Flash, Shockwave, ActiveX, Silverlight, NACL, or client-side Java applets
User-set passwords are at least 12 characters in length (or 15 for privileged accounts per DUC v4.3)
Passwords of at least 64 characters are permitted and that passwords of more than 128 characters are denied
A "change password" function is included that requires the current and new password
There are no password composition rules limiting the type of characters permitted
The user can choose to temporarily view the entire masked password, or temporarily view the last typed character
Anti-automation controls are effective at mitigating breached credential testing, brute force, and account lockout attacks
The use of weak authenticators (such as SMS and email) is limited to secondary verification and transaction approval
Secure notifications are sent to users after updates to authentication details
**[ENV-VAR BYPASS RULE — NEVER WAIVED]** Verify that anti-automation, lockout, rate limiting, and brute force controls are not bypassable via environment variables, configuration flags, or feature…
Credential providers and external credential partners are trusted and kept up to date
System generated initial passwords or activation codes are at least 6 characters long, contain letters and numbers, and expire after a short period of time
Enrollment and other high-value actions complete within a time period that has an appropriate expiry
Password hints or knowledge-based authentication (so-called "secret questions") are not present
Passwords are stored in a form that is resistant to offline attacks, using an approved adaptive hashing or salted hashing (bcrypt, scrypt, argon2, PBKDF2)
The salt is at least 32 bits in length and chosen arbitrarily to minimize salt value collisions among stored hashes
If PBKDF2 is used, the iteration count is as large as verification server performance will allow, typically at least 100,000 iterations
If bcrypt is used, the work factor is as large as verification server performance will allow, with a minimum of 10
An additional iteration of a key derivation function is performed, using a salt value that is secret and known only to the verifier
A system generated initial activation secret or recovery secret is not sent in clear text to the user
Password hints or knowledge-based recovery (secret questions) are not present
OTP or multi-factor recovery, refresh, or bypass codes are exchanged using a secure channel
Forgotten password and other recovery paths use a TOTP or other soft token, push notification, or other offline recovery mechanism
If an authentication factor is changed or replaced, the user is notified of this event
Forgotten password and other recovery paths use a time-limited one-time reset link, OTP, or another secret
OTP factors, where used, are invalidated after use
Look-up secrets have sufficient randomness of at least 112 bits of entropy
Look-up secrets are resistant to offline attacks, such as predictable values
Look-up secrets are salted and hashed using an approved one-way hash
Clear text out-of-band (NIST "restricted") authenticators, such as SMS or PSTN, are not offered by default, and that stronger alternatives such as push notifications are offered first
The out-of-band verifier expires out-of-band authentication requests after 10 minutes
The out-of-band authenticator is used at most once
Time-based OTPs have a defined lifetime before expiring
The keys used for time-based OTPs are stored securely using platform secrets storage
Approved cryptographic algorithms are used in the generation, seeding, and verification of OTPs
Time-based OTP can only be used once within the validity period
If a time-based multi-factor OTP token is re-used during the validity period, it is logged and the user is alerted
Physical single-factor OTP generators can be revoked in case of theft or loss
Cryptographic keys used in verification are stored securely and protected against disclosure, such as using a TPM, HSM, or OS keystore
The challenge nonce is at least 64 bits in length, and statistically unique or unique over the lifetime of the cryptographic device
Approved cryptographic algorithms are used in the generation, seeding, and verification
Integration secrets do not rely on unchanging passwords, such as API keys or shared secrets, except with legacy systems
If passwords are required for service authentication, the credentials used are not a default password
Passwords are stored with sufficient protection to prevent offline recovery attacks, including local system access
Passwords, integrations with databases and third-party systems, seeds and internal secrets, and API keys are managed securely and not included in source code or stored within source code repositories
The application never reveals session tokens in URL parameters or error messages
The application generates a new session token on user authentication and can be revoked, and that existing sessions are invalidated upon authentication
The application generates a new session token on user authentication
Session tokens possess at least 64 bits of entropy
The application only stores session tokens in the browser using secure methods
Session token are generated using approved cryptographic algorithms
Logout and expiration invalidate the session token, such that the back button or a downstream relying party does not resume an authenticated session, including across relying parties
Provides a mechanism to allow users to log out of any or all currently active sessions
Gives the option to terminate all other active sessions after a successful password change
Users are able to view and (optionally) log out any or all currently active sessions and devices
Cookie-based session tokens have the `Secure` attribute set
Cookie-based session tokens have the `HttpOnly` attribute set to prevent JavaScript access
Cookie-based session tokens utilize the `SameSite` attribute to limit exposure to cross-site request forgery attacks
Cookie-based session tokens use the `__Host-` prefix to provide cookie isolation
If the application is published under a domain name with other applications that set or use session cookies that might disclose the session cookies, set the path attribute as precisely as possible
The application allows users to revoke OAuth tokens that form trust relationships with linked applications
The application uses session tokens rather than static API secrets and keys, except with legacy integrations
Stateless session tokens use digital signatures, encryption, and other countermeasures to protect against tampering, enveloping, replay, null cipher, and key substitution attacks
The application ensures a full, valid login session or requires re-authentication or secondary verification before allowing any sensitive transactions or account changes
Enforces access control rules on a trusted service layer, especially if client-side access control is present and could be bypassed
All user and data attributes and policy information used by access controls cannot be manipulated by end users unless specifically authorized
The principle of least privilege exists — users should only be able to access functions, data files, URLs, controllers, services, and other resources, for which they possess specific authorization
The principle of deny by default exists whereby new users/roles start with minimal or no permissions and users/roles do not receive access to new features until access is explicitly assigned
Access controls fail securely including when an exception occurs
Middleware ordering enforces authentication before other security-relevant processing for protected routes

Sensitive data and APIs are protected against Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) / Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) attacks that target creation, reading, updating and deleting of records
Or framework enforces a strong anti-CSRF mechanism to protect authenticated functionality, and effective anti-automation or anti-CSRF protects unauthenticated functionality
Administrative interfaces use appropriate multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized use
Directory browsing is disabled unless deliberately desired
The application does not allow discovery or disclosure of file or directory metadata, such as Thumbs.db, .DS_Store, .git, or .svn folders
Has defenses against HTTP parameter pollution attacks, particularly if the application framework makes no distinction about the source of request parameters
Frameworks protect against mass parameter assignment attacks, or that the application has countermeasures to protect against unsafe parameter assignment
All input (HTML form fields, REST requests, URL parameters, HTTP headers, cookies, batch files, RSS feeds, etc.) is validated using positive validation (allow lists)
Structured data is strongly typed and validated against a defined schema including allowed characters, length and pattern
URL redirects and forwards only allow destinations which appear on an allowlist, or show a warning when redirecting to potentially untrusted content
All untrusted HTML input from WYSIWYG editors or similar is properly sanitized with an HTML sanitizer library or framework feature
Unstructured data is sanitized to enforce safety measures such as allowed characters and length
Sanitizes user input before passing to mail systems to protect against SMTP or IMAP injection
Avoids the use of `eval()` or other dynamic code execution features
Protects against template injection attacks by ensuring that any user input being included is sanitized or sandboxed
Protects against SSRF attacks, by validating or sanitizing untrusted data or HTTP file metadata
Sanitizes, disables, or sandboxes user-supplied SVG scriptable content
Sanitizes, disables, or sandboxes user-supplied scriptable or expression template language content
Output encoding is relevant for the interpreter and context required, such as encoding HTML entities for HTML content, URL encoding for URL parameters, attribute encoding for CSS or URL, etc
Output encoding preserves the user's chosen character set and locale, such that any Unicode character point is valid and safely handled
Context-aware, preferably automated output escaping protects against reflected, stored, and DOM XSS
Data selection or database queries (e.g., SQL, HQL, ORM, NoSQL) use parameterized queries, ORMs, entity frameworks or are otherwise protected from database injection attacks

Where parameterized or safer mechanisms are not present, context-specific output encoding is used to protect against injection attacks, such as the use of SQL escaping to protect against SQL Injection
Protects against JavaScript or JSON injection attacks, including for eval attacks and remote JavaScript includes
Protects against LDAP injection vulnerabilities, or that specific security controls to prevent LDAP injection have been implemented
Protects against OS command injection and that operating system calls use parameterized OS queries or use contextual command line output encoding
Protects against Local File Inclusion (LFI) or Remote File Inclusion (RFI) attacks
Protects against XPath injection or XML injection attacks
Uses memory-safe string, buffer, and integer operations for managed/unmanaged code
Format strings do not take potentially hostile input
Sign, range, and input validation techniques are used to prevent integer overflows
Serialized objects use integrity checks or are encrypted to prevent hostile object creation or data tampering
Correctly restricts XML parsers to only use the most restrictive configuration possible and to ensure that unsafe features such as resolving external entities are disabled to prevent XML External…
Deserialization of untrusted data is avoided or is protected in both custom code and third-party libraries
When parsing JSON in browsers or JavaScript-based backends, `JSON.parse` is used to parse the JSON document
Regulated private data is stored encrypted while at rest, such as Personally Identifiable Information (PII), sensitive personal information, or data assessed as likely to be subject to applicable…
Regulated health data is stored encrypted while at rest, such as medical records, medical device details, or de-anonymized research records

Regulated financial data is stored encrypted while at rest, such as financial accounts, defaults or credit history, tax records, pay history, beneficiaries, or de-anonymized market or research records
All cryptographic modules fail securely, and errors are handled in a way that does not enable Padding Oracle attacks
Industry-proven or government-approved cryptographic algorithms, modes, and libraries are used, rather than custom-developed cryptography
Encryption initialization vector, cipher configuration, and block modes are configured securely using the latest advice
Random number, encryption or hashing algorithms, key lengths, rounds, ciphers or modes, can be reconfigured, upgraded, or swapped at any time, to protect against cryptographic breaks
Known insecure block modes (i.e., ECB) are not used
Nonces, initialization vectors, and other single-use numbers must not be used more than once with a given encryption key
Encrypted data is authenticated via signatures, authenticated cipher modes or HMAC to ensure that ciphertext is not altered by an unauthorized party
All cryptographic operations are constant-time, with no 'short-circuit' operations in comparisons, key derivations, or encryptions to avoid information leakage
All random numbers, random file names, random GUIDs, and random strings are generated using the cryptographic module's approved cryptographically secure random number generator when these random…
Random GUIDs are created using the GUID v4 algorithm, and a Cryptographically-secure Pseudorandom Number Generator (CSPRNG)
Random numbers are created with proper entropy even when the application is under heavy load, or that the application degrades gracefully in such circumstances
A secrets management solution such as a key vault is used to securely create, store, control access to and destroy secrets
Key material is not exposed to the application but instead uses an isolated security module like a vault for cryptographic operations
Does not log credentials or payment details
Does not log other sensitive personal data that could lead to applicable privacy legislation implications
Logs security relevant events including successful and failed authentication events, access control failures, deserialization failures and input validation failures
Each log event includes necessary information that would allow for a detailed investigation when an incident occurs
All authentication decisions are logged, without storing sensitive session tokens or passwords
All access control decisions can be logged and all failed decisions are logged
All logging components appropriately encode data to prevent log injection
All events are protected from injection when viewed in log viewing software
Security logs are protected from unauthorized access and modification
Time sources are synchronized to the correct time and time zone
A generic message is shown when an unexpected or security sensitive error occurs, potentially with a unique ID which support personnel can use to investigate
Exception handling is used across the codebase to account for expected and unexpected error conditions
A "last resort" error handler is defined which will catch all unhandled exceptions
The application protects sensitive data from being cached in server components such as load balancers and application caches
All cached or temporary copies of sensitive data stored on the server are protected from unauthorized access or purged/invalidated after the authorized user accesses the sensitive data
The application minimizes the number of parameters in a request, such as hidden fields, Ajax variables, cookies and header values
The application can detect and alert on abnormal numbers of requests, such as by IP, user, total per hour or day, or whatever makes sense for the application
Regular backups of important data are performed and that test restoration of data is performed
Sensitive information contained in memory is overwritten as soon as it is no longer required to mitigate memory dumping attacks
Sensitive or private information that is required to be encrypted, is encrypted using approved cryptographic algorithms that provide both confidentiality and integrity
Cloud storage and object storage access policies protect sensitive data from unauthorized access
The application sets sufficient anti-caching headers so that sensitive data is not cached in modern browsers
Data stored in browser storage (localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, or cookies) does not contain sensitive data or PII
Authenticated data is cleared from client storage, such as the browser DOM, after the client or session is terminated
Sensitive data is sent to the server in the HTTP message body or headers, and that query string parameters from any HTTP verb do not contain sensitive data
Users have a method to remove or export their data on demand
Users are provided clear language regarding collection and use of supplied personal information and that users have provided opt-in consent for the use of that data before it is used in any way
All sensitive data created and processed by the application has been identified, and ensure that a policy exists on how to deal with sensitive data
Accessing sensitive data is audited (without logging the sensitive data itself), if the data is collected under relevant data protection directives or where logging of access is required
Sensitive information contained in memory is overwritten as soon as it is no longer required
Sensitive or private information that is required to be encrypted, is encrypted using approved cryptographic algorithms that provide both confidentiality and integrity
Sensitive personal information is subject to data retention classification, such that old or out of date data is deleted automatically, on a schedule, or as the situation requires
TLS is used for all client connectivity, and does not fall back to insecure or unencrypted protocols
Only the latest recommended versions of the TLS protocol are enabled, such as TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3
Only the latest recommended cipher suites are enabled, with the weakest cipher suites removed
Connections to and from the server use trusted TLS certificates
Encrypted communications such as TLS are used for all inbound and outbound connections, including for management ports, monitoring, authentication, API, or web service calls
All encrypted connections to external systems that involve sensitive information or functions are authenticated
Proper certification revocation, such as Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) Stapling, is enabled and configured
Backend TLS connection failures are logged
A malware scanning tool is used during build or CI/CD to detect potentially malicious code in sourced or developed code, third-party libraries, or external content
Source code and third-party libraries do not contain unauthorized phone home or data collection capabilities
Does not ask for unnecessary or excessive permissions
Source code and third-party libraries do not contain back doors, such as hardcoded or undocumented accounts or keys, code obfuscation, undocumented binary blobs, rootkits, or anti-debug, unsafe…
Source code and third-party libraries do not contain Easter eggs or any other potentially unwanted functionality
Source code and third-party libraries do not contain time bombs by searching for date and time related functions
Source code and third-party libraries do not contain malicious code that specifically searches for or attempts to steal passwords, access tokens, personal, or authentication credentials
If the application has a client or server auto-update feature, updates should be obtained over secure channels and digitally signed
Employs integrity protections, such as code signing or subresource integrity
**[AI/Bot Code Provenance — ALWAYS VERIFY]** Verify that code generated by AI coding tools or CI/CD automation (e.g., GitHub Copilot, GPT Engineer, Cursor, Codegen bots, Dependabot) was…
The application will only process business logic flows for the same user in sequential step order and without skipping steps
The application will only process business logic flows with all steps being processed in realistic human time, i.e., transactions are not submitted too quickly
The application has appropriate limits for specific business actions or transactions, and that these limits are correctly enforced on a per-user basis
The application has anti-automation controls to protect against excessive calls such as mass data exfiltration, business logic requests, file uploads or denial of service attacks
The application has business logic limits or validation to protect against likely business risks or threats, identified using threat modeling or similar methodologies
The application does not suffer from "time of check to time of use" (TOCTOU) problems or other race conditions for sensitive operations
The application monitors for unusual events or activity from a business logic perspective
The application has configurable alerting when automated attacks or unusual activity is detected
Will not accept large files that could fill up storage or cause a denial of service
Compressed files are checked for "zip bombs" — small files that will decompress into huge amounts of data thus exhausting file storage
A file size quota and maximum number of files per user is enforced to ensure that a single user cannot fill the storage with too many files
Files obtained from untrusted sources are validated to be of expected type based on the file's content
User-submitted filenames are not used directly in file system or framework operations, and that a URL parser is used to prevent path traversal attacks
User-submitted filenames are validated or ignored to prevent the disclosure, creation, updating or removal of local files (LFI)
User-submitted filenames are validated or ignored to prevent the disclosure, creation, updating or removal of remote files (RFI)
Protects against Reflective File Download (RFD) by validating or ignoring user-submitted filenames in a JSON, JSONP, or URL parameter, the response Content-Type header should be set to text/plain,…
Untrusted file metadata is not used directly with system APIs or libraries to protect against OS command injection
Does not include and execute functionality from untrusted sources
Files obtained from untrusted sources are stored outside the web root, with limited permissions
Files obtained from untrusted sources are scanned by antivirus scanners to prevent upload and serving of known malicious content
File upload paths are not served from the same hostname and port as the main application
Cloud storage bucket policies restrict access to authorized users and prevent unauthenticated public access, public listing, or public write operations
The web tier is configured to serve only files with specific file extensions to prevent unintentional information and source code leakage
Direct requests to uploaded files will never be executed as HTML or JavaScript content
The web or application server is configured with an allowlist of resources or systems to which the server can send requests or load data/files from
All application components use the same encodings and parsers to avoid parsing attacks that exploit different URI or file parsing behavior that could be used in SSRF and RFI attacks
Access to administration and management functions is limited to authorized administrators
API URLs do not expose sensitive information, such as the API key, session tokens etc
Authorization decisions are made at both the URI, enforced by programmatic or declarative security at the controller level, and at the resource or object level, enforced by model-based permissions
Requests containing unexpected or missing content types are rejected with appropriate headers (HTTP response status 406 Unacceptable or 415 Unsupported Media Type)
Enabled RESTful HTTP methods are a valid choice for the user or action, such that normal users cannot use DELETE or PUT on protected API or resources, such as `DELETE /users/{id}` or `PUT…
JSON schema validation is in place and verified before accepting input
RESTful web services that utilize cookies are protected from Cross-Site Request Forgery via the use of at least one or more of the following
REST services are protected from Denial of Service (DoS) using rate limiting or other anti-DoS mitigations
XSD schema validation takes place to ensure a properly formed XML document, followed by validation of each input field before any processing of that data takes place
The message payload is signed using WS-Security to ensure reliable transport between client and service
A query allowlist or a combination of depth limiting and amount limiting is used to prevent GraphQL or data layer expression Denial of Service (DoS) attacks
GraphQL or other data layer authorization logic is implemented at the business logic layer instead of the GraphQL layer
All API endpoints are protected using either authentication, authorization, or rate limiting
JWT tokens are validated as per the V3.5.3 requirements
API keys do not provide long-lived unrestricted access
The API has rate limiting enabled and that rate limiting is applied to both authenticated and unauthenticated users
Resource-intensive operations are protected by rate limits to prevent financial or resource abuse
OpenAPI or API documentation is not exposed in production, or if it must be, that access is restricted to authorized users
An API inventory exists and is maintained for all APIs
Inter-service calls use the minimum necessary authentication and authorization
The build pipeline does not include any hardcoded credentials, tokens, or API keys
Build and deployment configurations do not contain any development, test, or debug settings that are enabled in production builds
Debug features are disabled in production, including developer tools, debug consoles, or stack traces exposed to end users
All third-party components and libraries are from pre-defined, trusted sources and are kept up to date
An inventory of all third-party libraries is maintained and available
Unused dependencies, unnecessary features, components, files, and documentation are removed
Web or application server and framework error messages do not expose any unintended information (e.g., stack traces, server-side technology, or internal component names)
Web or application server and framework versions are not disclosed in HTTP headers or pages
Health check endpoints return only minimum information required by the load balancer or orchestrator
Authentication status endpoints do not expose internal implementation details (e.g., auth driver names, session store types, provider configuration)
Every HTTP response contains a Content-Type header with a safe character set (e.g., UTF-8, ISO 8859-1)
A Content Security Policy (CSP) response header is in place with a policy that helps mitigate common DOM, XSS, and JavaScript injection attacks
All responses contain an X-Content-Type-Options header with value `nosniff`
HTTP Strict Transport Security headers are included on all responses and for all subdomains
The content of a web application cannot be embedded in a third-party site by default and that embedding of the exact resources is only allowed where necessary using appropriate…
Server only accepts HTTP methods in use by the application and rejects unexpected methods (405 Method Not Allowed)
The supplied Origin header is not used for authentication or access control decisions, as the Origin header can easily be changed by an attacker
The cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) Allow-Origin header uses a strict allowlist of trusted domains and does not support the "null" origin
HTTP headers added by a trusted proxy or SSO devices, such as a bearer token, are authenticated by the application
blocks.caption: The complete requirement-by-requirement checklist Blue walks against every application. Requirement ids group by chapter (V2 = authentication, V5 = input validation, and so on).
blocks.source: AIDE common harness — control inventory, audited from source.

blocks.text: This inventory is not a description of the harness — it is the harness. Every check above is runnable code or a model skill in the open-source common harness, free for any government or organization to take, run, and extend. Browse and download it from our [repositories](/repos).
